title: Anaphylaxis
section: The Immune System in Health and Disease

items.title: Understanding the Biphasic Nature of Anaphylaxis
items.content: Anaphylactic reactions can be biphasic, where symptoms return after an initial resolution. This recurrence typically happens about an hour or longer after the first episode resolves.
keyPoints: 80–90% of anaphylactic episodes are uniphasic.
10–20% of cases are biphasic with a recurrence of symptoms occurring about an hour or longer after initial resolution.

items.title: Recognizing Severe Anaphylactic Reactions and Their Consequences
items.content: Severe anaphylaxis can lead to life-threatening conditions such as hypotension, hypoxia, cardiovascular collapse, or respiratory failure. These complications are often due to airway obstruction or vascular collapse.
keyPoints: Hypotension or hypoxia during anaphylaxis can lead to cardiovascular collapse or respiratory failure.
Upper or lower airway obstruction may occur, with laryngeal edema presenting as a 'lump' in the throat, hoarseness, or stridor.
Bronchial obstruction is associated with chest tightness and/or audible wheezing.
Patients with underlying asthma are at increased risk of severe lower airway involvement and higher mortality.
Fatal cases may show marked hyperinflation of lungs due to intractable bronchospasm.
Angioedema can cause death by mechanical obstruction, notably in the epiglottis and larynx.

items.title: Mechanisms of Anaphylaxis: Degranulation and Mediator Release
items.content: Anaphylaxis involves rapid degranulation of mast cells, basophils, and eosinophils, releasing mediators such as histamine, tryptase, chymase, heparin, and arachidonic acid-derived products. These mediators cause symptoms like flushing, urticaria, pruritus, hypotension, tachycardia, bronchoconstriction, and increased microvascular permeability.
keyPoints: Mast cells release histamine, proteases (tryptase, chymase), proteoglycans (heparin, chondroitin sulfate), tumor necrosis factor-α upon degranulation.
Histamine causes flushing, urticaria, pruritus, hypotension, and tachycardia.
Cysteinyl leukotrienes and prostaglandin D2 cause bronchoconstriction and increased microvascular permeability.
Serum PAF levels correlate with anaphylaxis severity and are inversely proportional to PAF acetylhydrolase levels.

items.title: Role of Tryptase and Chymase in Anaphylaxis: Complement and Coagulation Activation
items.content: Tryptase and chymase, released during anaphylaxis, activate complement and coagulation pathways. This activation leads to the production of anaphylatoxins (C3a, C5a) and stimulates the kallikrein-kinin system, affecting blood pressure and vascular permeability.
keyPoints: Tryptase and chymase activate complement and coagulation pathways.
Activation results in production of anaphylatoxins (C3a, C5a).
Kallikrein-kinin system activation regulates blood pressure and vascular permeability.
Mediator actions are additive or synergistic at target tissues.

items.title: Importance of Baseline Tryptase in Hymenoptera Sting-Induced Anaphylaxis
items.content: Baseline tryptase levels should be checked in patients presenting with sting-induced anaphylaxis to screen for systemic mastocytosis, as severe reactions are often the initial presentation.
keyPoints: Severe Hymenoptera-induced anaphylaxis can indicate underlying systemic mastocytosis.
Baseline tryptase reflects mast cell burden and aids in screening.

items.title: Risk of IgE-Mediated Drug Allergies with Repeated Exposure
items.content: Repeated exposure to certain drugs increases the risk of IgE-mediated hypersensitivity, notably in antibiotics like carboplatin where incidence rises significantly with more infusions.
keyPoints: IgE-mediated drug allergies are common with antibiotics and some chemotherapy drugs.
Incidence of hypersensitivity to carboplatin is 27% after ≥7 lifetime infusions and up to 46% after ≥15 infusions.

items.title: Non-IgE-Mediated Anaphylaxis Mechanisms
items.content: Anaphylaxis can occur via non-IgE mechanisms, such as drug-induced mast cell activation through MRGPRX2 or complement activation by Cremophor in paclitaxel.
keyPoints: Non-IgE-mediated anaphylaxis does not require prior sensitization.
MRGPRX2 can induce mast cell activation with drugs like NMBDs and quinolones.
Cremophor activates complement, causing histamine release in paclitaxel.

items.title: Understanding Non-IgE-Mediated Anaphylaxis: Scombroid Poisoning and Heparin Contamination
items.content: Non-IgE-mediated anaphylaxis can occur through mechanisms such as scombroid poisoning from histamine in fish or heparin contamination with oversulfated chondroitin sulfate (OSCS). These conditions do not elevate tryptase levels, distinguishing them from IgE-mediated reactions.
keyPoints: Scombroid poisoning is caused by ingestion of histamine from contaminated fish like mahi-mahi, mackerel, and tuna. Symptoms include flushing, oropharyngeal pruritis/angioedema, nausea/vomiting, lightheadedness, treatable with antihistamines and epinephrine.
Heparin contamination with OSCS activates the contact activation system, leading to anaphylaxis without elevated tryptase levels. Heparin is now screened for this contaminant.
Tryptase levels are not elevated in non-IgE-mediated reactions such as scombroid poisoning and heparin contamination.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Diagnosing Anaphylaxis: Timing, Testing, and Tryptase Levels
items.content: Anaphylaxis is diagnosed based on symptom onset after exposure to a trigger. Serum tryptase is a key biomarker for mast cell degranulation, peaking 60–90 minutes post-onset and measurable up to 5 hours later.
keyPoints: Diagnosis relies on history of symptoms within seconds to minutes after exposure to a trigger.
Serum tryptase peaks 60-90 minutes after anaphylaxis onset, measurable for up to 5 hours.
The '20% + 2' rule: A tryptase level >20% above baseline plus 2 ng/mL indicates significant mast cell activation.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Interpreting Baseline Serum Tryptase and Identifying Risk Factors
items.content: Baseline serum tryptase levels vary widely, with conditions like hereditary alpha-tryptasemia (HAT) causing elevated levels. Understanding these variations is crucial for diagnosing anaphylaxis.
keyPoints: ~6% of Western populations have HAT, leading to elevated baseline serum tryptase.
High environmental allergen exposure can temporarily elevate baseline serum tryptase.
The '20% + 2' rule helps interpret tryptase levels in the context of anaphylaxis diagnosis.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Early Epinephrine Administration in Anaphylaxis
items.content: Prompt administration of epinephrine is crucial for treating anaphylaxis due to its rapid onset and potential severity. Intramuscular injection of 0.3–0.5 mL of 1:1000 (1 mg/mL) epinephrine should be administered as soon as symptoms appear, with repeated doses every 5-20 minutes if necessary.
keyPoints: First-choice treatment is intramuscular epinephrine at a dose of 0.3–0.5 mL of 1:1000 (1 mg/mL).
Repeating doses can be given every 5-20 minutes as needed for severe reactions.
Failure to use epinephrine within the first 20 minutes is associated with poor clinical outcomes.

items.title: Management of Severe Hypotension and Respiratory Compromise in Anaphylaxis
items.content: In cases where anaphylactic reactions lead to severe hypotension or respiratory compromise, additional interventions are necessary. IV fluids and vasopressors may be used for persistent hypotension, while oxygen delivery methods should escalate to endotracheal intubation or tracheostomy if progressive hypoxia occurs.
keyPoints: IV fluids and vasopressors can be administered for intractable hypotension.
Oxygen via nasal catheter or nebulized albuterol may help initially, but endotracheal intubation or tracheostomy is required for severe hypoxia.
Epinephrine's effects include vasoconstriction and bronchial smooth-muscle relaxation.

items.title: Adjunctive Therapies Post-Epinephrine in Anaphylaxis
items.content: Once the patient is hemodynamically stable, adjunctive treatments such as antihistamines, glucocorticoids, and bronchodilators can be used to manage urticaria/angioedema and bronchospasm.
keyPoints: Antihistamines, glucocorticoids, and bronchodilators are useful once hemodynamic stability is achieved.
These agents help treat residual symptoms like urticaria/angioedema and bronchospasm.

items.question: Which of the following is a common initial clinical manifestation of anaphylaxis?
items.explanation: Cutaneous manifestations are among the most common presentations of anaphylaxis (>90% of cases).

items.question: What percentage of anaphylactic episodes are typically uniphasic?
items.explanation: While 80–90% of anaphylactic episodes are uniphasic.

items.question: Which organ system involvement in anaphylaxis can lead to cardiovascular collapse?
items.explanation: Anaphylactic reactions are particularly dangerous when hypotension or hypoxia occurs, leading potentially to cardiovascular collapse.

items.question: Which cells release preformed granules containing histamine and proteases upon activation?
items.explanation: Mast cells contain preformed granules with histamine and proteases that are released upon activation.

items.question: What mediators cause bronchoconstriction and increased microvascular permeability during anaphylaxis?
items.explanation: Cysteinyl leukotrienes and prostaglandin D2 are responsible for bronchoconstriction and increased microvascular permeability.

items.question: Which factor is inversely proportional to the severity of anaphylaxis?
items.explanation: The constitutive level of PAF acetylhydrolase, necessary for PAF inactivation, is inversely proportional to anaphylaxis severity.

items.question: Which condition is most commonly associated with severe Hymenoptera-induced anaphylaxis as the initial presentation?
items.explanation: Severe Hymenoptera-induced anaphylaxis is most commonly the initial presentation for patients with underlying systemic mastocytosis.

items.question: Which of the following occupations increases the likelihood of Hymenoptera allergy?
items.explanation: Occupations that place individuals in regular proximity to stinging insects, such as beekeepers, increase the likelihood of Hymenoptera allergy.

items.question: What is a common mechanism for non-IgE-mediated mast cell activation?
items.explanation: Non-IgE-mediated mast cell activation can occur through the MRGPRX2 receptor, as seen with certain drugs.

items.question: Which drug is associated with a lower rate of hypersensitivity when solubilized by albumin nanoparticles?
items.explanation: Abraxane, a version of paclitaxel solubilized by albumin nanoparticles, has a lower rate of hypersensitivity compared to Cremophor-solubilized paclitaxel.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT associated with elevated tryptase levels in anaphylaxis?
options: Anaphylaxis to IV unfractionated heparin contaminated with OSCS
items.explanation: Tryptase levels are not elevated in scombroid poisoning because the source of histamine is exogenous.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: Which biomarker has a short serum half-life and an expiration time-window for measurement within 1 hour from the onset of anaphylaxis?
items.explanation: Histamine has a short serum half-life with a measurable time-window that expires within <1 hour from the onset of anaphylaxis.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the peak time for serum tryptase levels after the onset of anaphylaxis?
items.explanation: Serum tryptase peaks 60–90 minutes after the onset of anaphylaxis.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What does the '20% + 2' rule help to determine in the context of tryptase levels?
options: The normal range of baseline serum tryptase
A diagnosis for an acute event involving significant mast cell activation
The half-life of histamine in the blood
items.explanation: The '20% + 2' rule helps to determine a diagnosis for an acute event involving significant mast cell activation.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.question: What is the first-choice treatment for anaphylaxis?
items.explanation: Epinephrine is the first-choice treatment for anaphylaxis due to its rapid action on alpha- and beta-adrenergic receptors.

items.question: What is a risk factor for poor clinical outcomes in anaphylaxis?
options: Delayed administration of epinephrine within the first 20 minutes
Use of beta blockers prior to anaphylaxis
Administration of oxygen via nasal catheter
items.explanation: Failure to use epinephrine within the first 20 minutes is a risk factor for poor clinical outcomes.

items.question: Which agents may be considered if beta blockers are present in anaphylaxis management?
items.explanation: An alternative antihypertensive may be considered in patients at high risk of needing emergency epinephrine if beta blockers are present.

items.statement: Biphasic anaphylactic reactions occur in about 80-90% of cases.
items.explanation: About 10–20% of anaphylactic cases are biphasic.

items.statement: Patients with underlying asthma have increased mortality associated with anaphylaxis.
items.explanation: Patients with underlying asthma are predisposed to severe involvement of the lower airways and increased mortality associated with anaphylaxis.

items.statement: Histamine release can result in hypotension and tachycardia.
items.explanation: High concentrations of histamine lead to hypotension and tachycardia.

items.statement: PAF levels correlate directly with the severity of anaphylaxis.
items.explanation: Serum PAF levels are inversely proportional to anaphylaxis severity.

items.statement: Atopy is a risk factor for anaphylaxis from drug reactions or Hymenoptera stings.
items.explanation: Atopy is not generally considered a risk factor for anaphylaxis from drug reactions or Hymenoptera stings.

items.statement: Repeated exposure to the allergy-causing antigen is important in evaluating patients with anaphylaxis.
items.explanation: Repeated exposure to the allergy-causing antigen is a significant risk factor for anaphylaxis.

items.statement: Histamine levels can be reliably measured up to several hours after the onset of anaphylaxis.
items.explanation: Histamine has a short serum half-life and is measurable only within <1 hour from the onset of anaphylaxis.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Tryptase levels can be used to document mast cell degranulation in anaphylactic episodes.
items.explanation: Serum tryptase is a biomarker released from mast cells and peaks after the onset of anaphylaxis, useful for documenting severity.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.statement: Epinephrine is administered intravenously as the first-line treatment for anaphylaxis.
items.explanation: Epinephrine is administered intramuscularly, not intravenously, as the first-line treatment.

items.statement: Oxygen alone is sufficient for managing progressive hypoxia in anaphylaxis.
items.explanation: Endotracheal intubation or tracheostomy is mandatory if progressive hypoxia develops.

items.assertion: Activation of the kallikrein-kinin system regulates blood pressure and vascular permeability.
items.reason: The actions of anaphylactic mediators are likely additive or synergistic at target tissues.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason does not explain why the kallikrein-kinin system regulates blood pressure and vascular permeability.

items.assertion: Patients with cystic fibrosis have a high incidence of allergic reactions to IV antibiotics.
items.reason: These patients receive periodic 'clean-outs' with beta-lactams for airway clearance.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: Alpha-gal syndrome is caused by IgE-mediated reactions to mammalian meat.
items.reason: Patients with a history of multiple bites from Amblyomma americanum ticks are more likely to have anti-alpha-gal IgE.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason does not explain the syndrome's mechanism; it only correlates tick exposure with anti-alpha-gal IgE.

items.assertion: Scombroid poisoning is treated with antihistamines and epinephrine.
items.reason: Scombroid poisoning involves the ingestion of histamine from contaminated fish, leading to symptoms that can be managed with these medications.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why antihistamines and epinephrine are used.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS

items.assertion: The diagnosis of anaphylaxis is primarily based on laboratory biomarkers.
items.reason: History revealing the onset of symptoms and signs after encountering a trigger is crucial for diagnosing anaphylaxis.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because history, not just lab biomarkers, is primary in diagnosis; the reason is true.
items.reference: Anaphylaxis · PREDISPOSING FACTORS AND MECHANISMS (part 2) | DIAGNOSIS
